perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall hold in trust and confidence, and not disclose to others, by any means, any and all INFORMATION disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall endeavor to hold in trust and confidence, and will attempt to not disclose to others, by any means, any and all INFORMATION disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall hold" with "shall endeavor to hold" and "not disclose" with "will attempt to not disclose" weakens the obligation of confidentiality, making it ambiguous and less enforceable. 'Endeavor' and 'attempt' suggest effort rather than a guarantee of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets through injunctive relief and damages for misappropriation. By weakening the confidentiality obligation, the modified text contradicts the Act's intent to provide strong protection against the disclosure or use of trade secrets. 'Endeavor' and 'attempt' do not meet the standard of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy as required to claim trade secret protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the confidentiality obligation by replacing "shall hold" with "shall endeavor to hold" and "not disclose" with "will attempt to not disclose." This introduces ambiguity regarding the receiving party's responsibility to maintain secrecy. The original text created a clear obligation, while the changed text suggests effort rather than a guaranteed outcome. According to Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' The change undermines the effort to maintain secrecy, and by extension, it weakens trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: INFORMATION may be used by the receiving party only for the purpose of considering or pursuing a business relationship or business transaction with the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: INFORMATION may be used by the receiving party for purposes related to considering or generally contemplating a potential business relationship or business transaction with the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'only for the purpose of considering or pursuing' to 'for purposes related to considering or generally contemplating' broadens the permissible uses of the information. The terms 'related to' and 'generally contemplating' are vague and allow for a broader interpretation of how the information can be used, potentially beyond the scope intended by the disclosing party. This introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation, which includes using the information for purposes outside the scope of the agreement. Broadening the permitted use with vague terms like 'related to' and 'generally contemplating' creates a loophole where the receiving party could potentially use the information in ways that harm the disclosing party, undermining CUTSA's protection of trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the permissible uses of information with vague terms, creating a contradiction. The original text restricts information use to 'considering or pursuing' a business relationship. The changed text expands this to 'related to considering or generally contemplating' which introduces ambiguity and undermines the protection of trade secrets. Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By broadening the scope of permitted use, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation by allowing the receiving party to use the information in ways not originally intended or consented to, potentially harming the disclosing party, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party further agrees it shall take the same measures, but no less than reasonable security measures, and use the same care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to preserve and protect the secrecy of, and to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of, the disclosing party's INFORMATION as it uses with its own information of similar importance.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party further agrees it will consider taking similar measures, striving for reasonable security measures, and will aim to use the same level of care, targeting a reasonable degree of care, to preserve and protect the secrecy of, and to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of, the disclosing party's INFORMATION as it uses with its own information of similar importance.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall take" with "will consider taking", "no less than reasonable security measures" with "striving for reasonable security measures", and "use the same care" with "will aim to use the same level of care" significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This creates ambiguity regarding the actual security measures required.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Replacing definitive obligations with aspirational language like 'striving for' and 'will aim to' undermines the requirement for concrete and effective security measures, making it more difficult to prove a violation of CUTSA if the information is misappropriated. The modifications create uncertainty regarding whether sufficient measures were actually taken.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The original text uses "shall take" and "use the same care," indicating a mandatory obligation. The changed text replaces these with "will consider taking" and "will aim to use the same level of care," introducing ambiguity and weakening the commitment. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this weakens the obligation. The Law Explanation specifies that under CUTSA, reasonable efforts are required to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, and aspirational language undermines concrete security measures. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' which includes disclosure or use of a trade secret and refers to 'a duty to maintain secrecy'. The change from a definitive obligation to an aspirational one directly contradicts the need for reasonable efforts and a duty to maintain secrecy, creating an ambiguity that makes the contract less enforceable and increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall, upon request of the disclosing party: (i) return to the disclosing party all documents, drawings, equipment and other tangible materials, including all INFORMATION and all manifestations thereof, delivered to the receiving party under this Agreement, and all copies and reproductions thereof; and (ii) certify to the disclosing party that all such INFORMATION has been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall, upon reasonable request of the disclosing party: (i) attempt to return to the disclosing party all documents, drawings, equipment and other tangible materials, including all INFORMATION and manifestations thereof, delivered to the receiving party under this Agreement, and copies and reproductions thereof to the extent reasonably possible; and (ii) provide confirmation to the disclosing party that all such INFORMATION has been addressed.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'reasonable' before 'request,' changing 'return' to 'attempt to return,' adding 'to the extent reasonably possible,' and changing 'certify' to 'provide confirmation that all such information has been addressed' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential information. 'Reasonable request' is subjective, 'attempt to return' is not a guarantee, 'to the extent reasonably possible' creates loopholes, and 'addressed' is less definitive than 'returned'.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA's remedies for misappropriation, including injunctive relief, are undermined if the contract allows the receiving party to avoid returning trade secrets through vague or discretionary obligations. By making the return of information less certain, the modification makes it more difficult for the disclosing party to recover its trade secrets and enforce its rights under CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret protection laws. The original text mandates the return of all information, while the changed text only requires an attempt to return information 'to the extent reasonably possible' and confirmation that the information has been 'addressed,' rather than returned. This weakens the obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which includes 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The modified contract language, by making the return of trade secrets less certain, increases the risk of misappropriation as it allows the receiving party more leeway in handling confidential information. This directly undermines the protections afforded by CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Receiving party shall adhere to the U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products received from the disclosure or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed countries.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Receiving party should generally be aware of the U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations and should consider whether to export or re-export any technical data or products received from the disclosure or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed countries.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall adhere to" with "should generally be aware of" and "shall not export or re-export" with "should consider whether to export or re-export" transforms a mandatory legal obligation into a discretionary suggestion. This ambiguity weakens the commitment to complying with export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR mandates strict compliance with export control laws and regulations. By making adherence discretionary ('should be aware of,' 'should consider'), the modified text creates a loophole where the receiving party could claim a lack of understanding or intent as a defense for violating export restrictions, directly contradicting the EAR's requirement for full and unwavering compliance.

perturbation.justification: The original text states the receiving party 'shall adhere' and 'shall not export or re-export,' indicating a mandatory obligation to comply with U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations. The changed text replaces these mandatory terms with 'should generally be aware of' and 'should consider whether to export or re-export,' transforming the obligation into a discretionary suggestion. This change directly contradicts the requirement for strict compliance as it introduces ambiguity, thus creating a legal contradiction as the commitment to comply with export control laws is weakened.
